definition.title: Kafka Checkout Consumer Lag
definition.summary: Checkout events are delayed because the payments consumer group is stuck behind a poison message after a schema change.
definition.root_cause: A backward-incompatible schema change introduced a poison message that repeatedly fails deserialization and stalls the payments consumer group.
definition.impact: Payment confirmation events are delayed by 18 to 24 minutes.

signals.name: consumer_lag_spike
signals.expected: Lag rises from 180 to more than 52000 offsets after 10:08 UTC.

signals.name: stable_broker_cpu
signals.expected: Broker CPU remains below 42 percent, reducing broker saturation likelihood.

red_herrings.clue: A checkout-api deploy completed four minutes before the page fired.
red_herrings.reason: The deploy only changed frontend validation and request volume stayed flat.

red_herrings.clue: A Kafka broker leader election occurred earlier in the hour.
red_herrings.reason: Partition ISR recovered before the lag spike and broker health is normal.

definition.runbook: Check consumer lag by group, inspect failed offsets, compare schema registry versions, and quarantine poison messages before restarting the consumer.

alert.title: Kafka Checkout Consumer Lag
alert.summary: Checkout events are delayed because the payments consumer group is stuck behind a poison message after a schema change.

logs.message: A Kafka broker leader election occurred earlier in the hour.
logs.why_not_root_cause: Partition ISR recovered before the lag spike and broker health is normal.

logs.why_not_root_cause: The deploy only changed frontend validation and request volume stayed flat.

logs.message: stable_broker_cpu: Broker CPU remains below 42 percent, reducing broker saturation likelihood.

runbook: Check consumer lag by group, inspect failed offsets, compare schema registry versions, and quarantine poison messages before restarting the consumer.

ground_truth.root_cause: A backward-incompatible schema change introduced a poison message that repeatedly fails deserialization and stalls the payments consumer group.
evidence_terms: Lag rises from 180 to more than 52000 offsets after 10:08 UTC.
Broker CPU remains below 42 percent, reducing broker saturation likelihood.
ground_truth.impact: Payment confirmation events are delayed by 18 to 24 minutes.

expected_signals.name: consumer_lag_spike
expected_signals.expected: Lag rises from 180 to more than 52000 offsets after 10:08 UTC.

expected_signals.name: stable_broker_cpu
expected_signals.expected: Broker CPU remains below 42 percent, reducing broker saturation likelihood.

red_herrings.clue: A checkout-api deploy completed four minutes before the page fired.
red_herrings.reason: The deploy only changed frontend validation and request volume stayed flat.

red_herrings.clue: A Kafka broker leader election occurred earlier in the hour.
red_herrings.reason: Partition ISR recovered before the lag spike and broker health is normal.

examples.strong: The root cause is A backward-incompatible schema change introduced a poison message that repeatedly fails deserialization and stalls the payments consumer group. The affected service is payments-consumer. Evidence: consumer_lag_spike — Lag rises from 180 to more than 52000 offsets after 10:08 UTC. Also, deserialize_errors — Decode errors repeat for checkout.payment_authorized.v7. Finally, stable_broker_cpu — Broker CPU remains below 42 percent, reducing broker saturation likelihood. Remediation: skip poison message, rollback schema, and add dead letter queue.
examples.fooled: The likely cause is checkout-api deploy and broker outage. The clue was: A checkout-api deploy completed four minutes before the page fired. We should roll back the most recent unrelated change and keep monitoring.

definition.title: Postgres Connection Pool Exhaustion
definition.summary: API latency and 5xx errors rise because workers leak database connections after a retry path skips pool release.
definition.root_cause: The orders-api retry path leaks Postgres connections when inventory lookups timeout, exhausting pgbouncer and causing request queuing.
definition.impact: Order creation p95 latency reaches 9.4 seconds and error rate reaches 11 percent.

signals.name: pgbouncer_waiting_clients
signals.expected: Waiting clients rise from 0 to 146 while active connections cap out.

signals.name: retry_timeout_logs
signals.expected: Retries continue after inventory timeouts without a matching pool release log.

signals.name: db_cpu_normal
signals.expected: Database CPU remains under 55 percent, pointing away from query saturation.

red_herrings.clue: A read replica reported replication lag.
red_herrings.reason: The failing write path uses the primary database, not the read replica.
Checkout order creation is not cacheable and bypasses CloudFront.

definition.runbook: Check pgbouncer pool state, compare active and waiting clients, inspect application retry paths, and verify release/finally blocks around database usage.

alert.title: Postgres Connection Pool Exhaustion
alert.summary: API latency and 5xx errors rise because workers leak database connections after a retry path skips pool release.

logs.why_not_root_cause: Checkout order creation is not cacheable and bypasses CloudFront.

logs.why_not_root_cause: The failing write path uses the primary database, not the read replica.

ground_truth.root_cause: The orders-api retry path leaks Postgres connections when inventory lookups timeout, exhausting pgbouncer and causing request queuing.
evidence_terms: Waiting clients rise from 0 to 146 while active connections cap out.
Retries continue after inventory timeouts without a matching pool release log.
Database CPU remains under 55 percent, pointing away from query saturation.
ground_truth.impact: Order creation p95 latency reaches 9.4 seconds and error rate reaches 11 percent.

expected_signals.name: pgbouncer_waiting_clients
expected_signals.expected: Waiting clients rise from 0 to 146 while active connections cap out.

expected_signals.name: retry_timeout_logs
expected_signals.expected: Retries continue after inventory timeouts without a matching pool release log.

expected_signals.name: db_cpu_normal
expected_signals.expected: Database CPU remains under 55 percent, pointing away from query saturation.

red_herrings.clue: A read replica reported replication lag.
red_herrings.reason: The failing write path uses the primary database, not the read replica.
Checkout order creation is not cacheable and bypasses CloudFront.

examples.strong: The root cause is The orders-api retry path leaks Postgres connections when inventory lookups timeout, exhausting pgbouncer and causing request queuing. The affected service is orders-api. Evidence: pgbouncer_waiting_clients — Waiting clients rise from 0 to 146 while active connections cap out. Also, retry_timeout_logs — Retries continue after inventory timeouts without a matching pool release log. Finally, db_cpu_normal — Database CPU remains under 55 percent, pointing away from query saturation. Remediation: patch retry path, release connection, and raise pool limit temporarily.
examples.fooled: The likely cause is replication lag and cache hit. The clue was: A read replica reported replication lag. We should roll back the most recent unrelated change and keep monitoring.

definition.title: Airflow DAG Backfill Storm
definition.summary: A data platform DAG floods the scheduler because a catchup flag was enabled during a deployment.
definition.root_cause: The revenue-etl DAG was deployed with catchup enabled and an old start_date, creating a backfill storm that starves current scheduled runs.
definition.impact: Revenue freshness SLA misses by 47 minutes for the EU warehouse.

signals.name: queued_dag_runs
signals.expected: Queued DAG runs jump from 2 to 318 after the deployment.

signals.name: catchup_config
signals.expected: Scheduler logs show catchup=True with start_date=2025-01-01.

signals.name: worker_capacity_ok
signals.expected: Workers have spare slots, so worker capacity is not the primary limiter.

red_herrings.clue: Snowflake warehouse queue time briefly increased.
red_herrings.reason: Warehouse queueing starts after the Airflow backlog and is downstream impact.
The warnings are non-fatal and appear in successful historical runs too.

definition.runbook: Inspect DAG config changes, queued run count, scheduler health, pool slots, and downstream warehouse queue time before clearing or pausing runs.

alert.title: Airflow DAG Backfill Storm
alert.summary: A data platform DAG floods the scheduler because a catchup flag was enabled during a deployment.

logs.why_not_root_cause: Warehouse queueing starts after the Airflow backlog and is downstream impact.

evidence_terms: Queued DAG runs jump from 2 to 318 after the deployment.
Scheduler logs show catchup=True with start_date=2025-01-01.
Workers have spare slots, so worker capacity is not the primary limiter.
ground_truth.impact: Revenue freshness SLA misses by 47 minutes for the EU warehouse.

expected_signals.name: queued_dag_runs
expected_signals.expected: Queued DAG runs jump from 2 to 318 after the deployment.

expected_signals.name: catchup_config
expected_signals.expected: Scheduler logs show catchup=True with start_date=2025-01-01.

expected_signals.name: worker_capacity_ok
expected_signals.expected: Workers have spare slots, so worker capacity is not the primary limiter.

red_herrings.clue: Snowflake warehouse queue time briefly increased.
red_herrings.reason: Warehouse queueing starts after the Airflow backlog and is downstream impact.
The warnings are non-fatal and appear in successful historical runs too.

examples.strong: The root cause is The revenue-etl DAG was deployed with catchup enabled and an old start_date, creating a backfill storm that starves current scheduled runs. The affected service is revenue-etl. Evidence: queued_dag_runs — Queued DAG runs jump from 2 to 318 after the deployment. Also, catchup_config — Scheduler logs show catchup=True with start_date=2025-01-01. Finally, worker_capacity_ok — Workers have spare slots, so worker capacity is not the primary limiter. Remediation: disable catchup, reset start_date, and clear queued backfills.
examples.fooled: The likely cause is snowflake queue and dbt model. The clue was: Snowflake warehouse queue time briefly increased. We should roll back the most recent unrelated change and keep monitoring.

definition.title: Redis Memory Fragmentation Spiral
definition.summary: Session reads intermittently fail because Redis memory fragmentation climbs after a burst of large temporary keys.
definition.root_cause: A burst of oversized preview-session keys caused Redis memory fragmentation and eviction pressure, leading to session cache misses.
definition.impact: Authenticated users see intermittent logout loops for 7 percent of requests.

signals.name: fragmentation_ratio
signals.expected: Fragmentation ratio climbs from 1.2 to 2.8 within twelve minutes.

signals.name: evicted_keys
signals.expected: Evictions begin immediately after preview-session key volume rises.

signals.name: large_key_logs
signals.expected: Profile preview writes payloads larger than the configured 64 KB budget.

red_herrings.clue: A mobile app release increased login traffic.
red_herrings.reason: Login traffic increased only 3 percent and cannot explain the eviction slope.

red_herrings.clue: TLS handshakes had a small latency bump.
red_herrings.reason: Handshake latency is after the cache miss and is not causal.

definition.runbook: Inspect Redis memory, fragmentation, key cardinality, largest keys, eviction counters, and recent writers before restarting or deleting keys.

expected_signals.expected: Profile preview writes payloads larger than the configured 64 KB budget.

red_herrings.clue: A mobile app release increased login traffic.
red_herrings.reason: Login traffic increased only 3 percent and cannot explain the eviction slope.

red_herrings.clue: TLS handshakes had a small latency bump.
red_herrings.reason: Handshake latency is after the cache miss and is not causal.

examples.strong: The root cause is A burst of oversized preview-session keys caused Redis memory fragmentation and eviction pressure, leading to session cache misses. The affected service is session-cache. Evidence: fragmentation_ratio — Fragmentation ratio climbs from 1.2 to 2.8 within twelve minutes. Also, evicted_keys — Evictions begin immediately after preview-session key volume rises. Finally, large_key_logs — Profile preview writes payloads larger than the configured 64 KB budget. Remediation: delete oversized keys, restart redis, and cap payload size.
examples.fooled: The likely cause is mobile release and certificate. The clue was: A mobile app release increased login traffic. We should roll back the most recent unrelated change and keep monitoring.
